title: Learn AI – Build and Deploy an AI Agent with Topcoder MCP on Hugging Face
summary: Design a comprehensive architecture for an AI Agent application that leverages the Topcoder Model Context Protocol (MCP) server and runs within a Hugging Face Space using CPU Basic tier. The architecture must support varied agent types (chatbot, automation, research assistant, code generator, visualization), ensure smooth MCP integration via SSE or Streamable HTTP, provide a user-friendly UI, and include deployment and documentation considerations.

artifacts.design_doc_md:
## Problem Summary
- Build an AI Agent leveraging Topcoder MCP server to solve a clear user problem.
- Agent runs in Hugging Face Space on CPU Basic tier with no GPU dependency.
- Must integrate with MCP via SSE or Streamable HTTP to access Topcoder APIs.
- Deliver a user-friendly, intuitive interface for interaction.

## Proposed Approach
- Use a modular architecture separating backend logic, MCP communication, and frontend UI.
- Backend implemented in Python or TypeScript, connecting to MCP server via SSE or HTTP streaming.
- Frontend built with Gradio for easy Hugging Face deployment and user interaction.
- Employ prompt engineering and response parsing to produce meaningful agent outputs.

## File-Level Plan
- `/backend/agent.py` or `/src/agent.ts`: Core logic connecting to MCP and processing data.
- `/frontend/app.py` or `/frontend/index.tsx`: UI components and user interaction handlers.
- `requirements.txt` or `package.json`: Dependency management.
- `README.md`: Setup, deployment, and usage instructions.
- `docs/use_case.md`: Description of problem and solution rationale.
- Optional `/tests/`: Unit and integration tests.

## API / Interface Changes
- Backend exposes a simple API or callable functions to frontend for sending user input.
- Backend connects to MCP server endpoints:
  - SSE: https://api.topcoder-dev.com/v6/mcp/sse
  - Streamable HTTP: https://api.topcoder-dev.com/v6/mcp/mcp
- Data formats: JSON request/response with streaming support.
- Frontend sends user queries to backend; backend streams MCP responses back for UI display.

## Constraints & SLAs
- Must run within Hugging Face Spaces CPU Basic environment (limited CPU, no GPU).
- MCP server is publicly accessible without authentication but may have rate limits.
- Response latency target: under 5 seconds for typical queries.
- UI must remain responsive with loading indicators during MCP streaming.

## Risks & Trade-offs
- SSE vs Streamable HTTP:
  - SSE provides real-time streaming but may have compatibility issues.
  - Streamable HTTP simpler but less real-time.
- Using Gradio simplifies UI but limits customization.
- MCP server downtime or network issues could degrade agent responsiveness.
- Resource constraints on Hugging Face may limit scalability.

## Edge Cases
- MCP server disconnects or timeouts mid-response.
- Malformed or unexpected MCP responses.
- User inputs invalid or unsupported queries.
- Network latency causing delayed or partial responses.

## Acceptance Checklist
- Agent connects reliably to MCP server and streams responses.
- UI allows user input and displays streamed output clearly.
- Agent addresses a well-defined use case with meaningful results.
- Application runs successfully on Hugging Face CPU Basic tier.
- Documentation includes setup, usage, and deployment instructions.
- Optional demo video showcases core functionality.
artifacts.mermaid_diagram:
graph TD
  User[User Interface (Gradio)] -->|User Input| Backend[Agent Backend]
  Backend -->|SSE or HTTP Stream| MCP[Topcoder MCP Server]
  MCP -->|Streamed Response| Backend
  Backend -->|Processed Output| User

interfaces.protocol: HTTP REST or local function call

interfaces.payload: Streaming JSON responses with partial results

tradeoffs: SSE provides efficient real-time streaming but may have browser compatibility and connection stability issues; Streamable HTTP is simpler but less interactive.
Gradio offers rapid UI development and smooth Hugging Face integration but limits UI customization compared to custom React or Vue apps.
Python backend is widely supported and easy for MCP SSE integration; TypeScript/Node.js offers better frontend/backend synergy but may add complexity.
Deploying on Hugging Face CPU Basic tier restricts resource-heavy models or parallelism but ensures free, accessible hosting.

assumptions: The AI agent will connect to the public Topcoder MCP server endpoints without authentication.
The agent must run on Hugging Face Spaces using CPU Basic tier, so GPU and heavy resource usage are disallowed.
The architecture should support extensibility for multiple agent types but focus on a single use case for clarity.
No detailed internal MCP API specification is provided; assume standard SSE or HTTP streaming protocols.
The UI will be implemented using Gradio or a similar lightweight framework compatible with Hugging Face Spaces.
Deployment and testing will be done on Hugging Face Spaces with typical CPU constraints.
Documentation and demo video are part of the final deliverables but outside the core architecture scope.
plan: Define the problem domain and use case for the AI agent, specifying the user problem and MCP tools utilized.
Design the overall system architecture including backend agent logic, MCP communication layer, and frontend UI.
Create a file-level plan organizing source code, configuration, and documentation for maintainability.
Specify API and interface contracts between UI, backend, and MCP server including transport protocols and data formats.
Enumerate constraints such as resource limits on Hugging Face Spaces, latency SLAs, and MCP server availability.
Identify risks such as MCP server downtime, network latency, and user input edge cases; propose mitigations.
Analyze trade-offs between SSE vs Streamable HTTP, UI frameworks, and deployment strategies.
Outline edge cases including MCP server disconnects, malformed responses, and invalid user inputs.
Develop an acceptance checklist covering functionality, stability, MCP integration, UI responsiveness, and documentation completeness.
validations: Verified architecture doc covers all required sections per rubric.
Mermaid diagram correctly represents component interactions.
Interfaces specify clear producer-consumer relationships and protocols.
Trade-offs and risks address key design decisions and constraints.
Acceptance checklist aligns with challenge submission criteria.

rubric_self_check.overall_notes: The architecture document fully addresses problem scope, design approach, file plan, interfaces, constraints, risks, edge cases, and acceptance criteria with clear, actionable details suitable for implementation and review.
